sweep.rule: A fact is stated only if >=2 independent sources agree. Pending/forward events are kept in the future tense. Every datapoint is tagged sourced or estimated.
sweep.note: Refreshed on the 22-Jun-2026 (Monday) sweep — US cash markets reopen today after the Fri 19-Jun Juneteenth close; the last full session was Thu 18-Jun (S&P 500 ~7,500, +0.9% on the holiday-shortened week, its 11th up-week in 12; VIX ~16). The regime is unchanged — a dollar melt-up on a hawkish-hold Fed: the FOMC held 3.50-3.75% on 17 Jun (Warsh's first meeting) but the dots turned hawkish — 9 of 18 officials now pencil a 2026 hike and the cutting cycle is pushed to 2027 — on a 4.2% May CPI, and DXY broke above 100. The yen is the pressure point: the BoJ hiked 25bp to 1% on 17 Jun, yet USD/JPY ran to a 160.89 high on 19 Jun (weakest yen since 11-Jul-2024) even after ~$70bn+ of MoF intervention, the wide differential keeping the carry trade dominant; spot sits ~160.8 in the MoF zone with Tokyo 'ready to act at any time'. EUR/USD is ~1.143 (the 11-Jun ECB hike to 2.25% overwhelmed by Fed hawkishness) and cable ~1.32. Brent is ~$78-80 (down ~8% on the week) after the 18-Jun interim US-Iran framework reopened Hormuz, though Geneva talks were postponed 20-Jun, keeping a re-escalation tail; gold is ~$4,180 (a third straight weekly fall on the strong dollar). The week's calendar: FedEx FQ4 (Tue 23-Jun) and Micron FQ3 (Wed 24-Jun) headline, with PCE on Thursday and NVIDIA's annual shareholder meeting. Implied moves are options-desk estimates (labelled estimated); consensus figures are flagged where not dual-confirmed.

earnings.tradeStatement: Long Micron (MU) into the 24-Jun FQ3 print — bullish on HBM / AI-memory pricing into 2027 — but expressed with a structure (autocall or collar), not naked stock, given the rich implied move.
earnings.name: Micron Technology
earnings.headline: Micron FQ3 — the AI-memory print of the quarter
earnings.thesis: Micron reports fiscal Q3 after the close on Wednesday 24 June — the week's marquee print. The Street expects explosive growth (revenue seen surging to roughly $35B, ~+276% YoY) as the memory supply/demand imbalance lifts pricing. But with HBM sold out for several quarters and 2026 capacity already booked, the print is less about the quarter just gone and more about 2027 pricing and the guide. A hard, dated binary for anyone holding the name — the kind of event you express with a structure rather than naked stock.
earnings.changeMyMind: Guidance pointing to 2027 HBM pricing rolling over, or the booked-capacity contracts being renegotiated — the 2027 pricing path, not the quarter just reported, is the thesis.

pillars.note: Implied straddle ~8.5% vs ~6.5% average realised over recent prints — the market is paying up for the move, so little edge buying the event.
Street broadly constructive on AI-memory; HBM demand underpins above-spot price targets.
Hard dated binary — FQ3 after the close on 24 Jun.
At/near an all-time high into the print — a notable crowded-long lean, not a short-squeeze setup.

earnings.watch: 2027 HBM pricing and the forward guide — not the quarter just reported.

facts.text: HBM sold out for several quarters; full-year 2026 HBM capacity booked under binding contracts
Reports FQ3 after the close on Wednesday 24 June; Street consensus revenue ~$35B (~+276% YoY) on the memory pricing surge
Stock ~$150, near a 52-week high into the print

earnings.tradeStatement: Long FedEx (FDX) into FQ4 — a bet the freight / industrial cycle is inflecting and the DRIVE cost program delivers; a global-trade read-through, not just the single stock.
earnings.name: FedEx
earnings.headline: FedEx FQ4 — the global-trade bellwether
earnings.thesis: FedEx reports fiscal Q4 after the close on Tuesday 23 June — consensus near $6.41 EPS on ~$24.0B revenue (+8% YoY). As a freight and global-trade bellwether it is a live read on the industrial cycle and tariff drag — useful well beyond the stock itself for any book carrying cyclicals or wanting to size the 'broadening beyond mega-caps' rotation.
earnings.changeMyMind: The FY27 guide cut on weak freight demand or tariff drag, or the DRIVE cost programme slipping — a deteriorating industrial cycle kills the broadening-rotation read.

pillars.note: Implied ~7% vs ~6% realised — modestly rich into a two-sided guide.
Cheap multiple but ratings are mixed; guidance risk cuts both ways.
Dated FQ4 on 23 Jun — a live read on the freight / industrial cycle.
Cyclical and sentiment-sensitive, but not at a positioning extreme.

earnings.watch: FY27 guide, freight demand, DRIVE cost programme and any tariff/trade commentary.

facts.text: FedEx reports FQ4 results after the close on Tuesday 23 June; consensus ~$6.41 EPS on ~$24.0B revenue (+8% YoY)
Treated as a global-trade / industrial-cycle bellwether

earnings.tradeStatement: Long Nike (NKE) into FQ4 — a single-name turnaround bet (inventory clean-up, China, the wholesale reset), not a sector or consumer-discretionary call.
earnings.name: Nike
earnings.headline: Nike FQ4 — a single-name turnaround bet
earnings.thesis: Nike reports fiscal Q4 after the close on 30 June. This is a company-specific turnaround story — inventory clean-up, China and the wholesale reset — rather than a desk theme. Lower conviction and off-theme, but a real catalyst for any book with consumer-discretionary exposure.
earnings.offThemeWhy: No standing desk theme on consumer discretionary — this is a single-name turnaround catalyst, judged on its own merit, not a strategic house view.
earnings.changeMyMind: Gross margins failing to recover or China deteriorating further — the turnaround thesis depends on margin and China inflecting, not the wholesale reset alone.

pillars.note: Implied ~8% vs ~7% realised — modestly rich for a turnaround print.
Street cautious on the multi-quarter reset; no clean buy-side alignment.
Dated FQ4 after the close on 30 Jun — a real single-name catalyst.
Beaten-down name with some short interest, but the turnaround keeps it from a clean squeeze setup.

earnings.watch: Gross-margin recovery, China trajectory and the FY27 revenue guide.

facts.text: Nike reports FQ4 fiscal-2026 results after the close on 30 June

earnings.tradeStatement: Buy the post-earnings dip in Oracle (ORCL) — a record Q4 (cloud +47%, OCI +93%, AI/RPO backlog surging) sold off ~5–7% purely on capex/funding fear ($55.7B FY26 capex vs a ~$50B guide); the reaction overshoots a thesis that is intact, so fade the drop and accumulate, with a stop below the post-print low.
earnings.name: Oracle
earnings.headline: Oracle beat a record quarter — and fell ~5–7% on capex fear. The dip looks buyable.
earnings.thesis: Oracle reported Q4 FY26 on 10 June: record revenue $19.2B (+21%), total cloud $9.9B (+47%) and OCI/IaaS $5.8B (+93%), with non-GAAP EPS $2.11 beating ~$1.89 and FY27 revenue guided to ~$90B on a surging AI/RPO backlog. The stock still fell ~5–7% — entirely on the funding of that growth: FY26 capex hit $55.7B (above the ~$50B guide) and gross margins dipped as data centres ramp. That is a price-reaction, not a thesis break: the capex IS the backlog. Play the post-print overshoot — buy the first-day dip and accumulate, stop below the reaction low; the risk is that AI-capex doubt persists and compresses the multiple further.

variant.street: Analysts raised price targets on the record cloud/RPO print despite the selloff
variant.us: The capex-driven drop overshoots — accumulate the dip; the buildout funds the backlog
variant.gap: Market is pricing capex as cost, not as the contracted-revenue engine it backs

earnings.changeMyMind: Evidence the AI-capex isn't converting — RPO/bookings stalling or OCI gross margins deteriorating further rather than ramping — would turn the dip into a value trap.

pillars.note: A ~5–7% drop on a record beat is a real dip-buy edge, but not a clean misprice — the capex concern is legitimate.
Sell-side raised price targets after the print; the cloud/RPO trajectory is broadly endorsed.
Dated, just-landed reaction (10 Jun) — a record print sold off, the classic post-print overshoot setup.
A 5–7% flush clears some weak longs, but AI-capex skeptics remain an overhang — not a washout.

earnings.result: Beat — record Q4 revenue $19.2B (+21%), total cloud $9.9B (+47%), OCI $5.8B (+93%); non-GAAP EPS $2.11 vs ~$1.89 est; FY27 revenue guided ~$90B
earnings.reaction: Shares fell ~5–7% after the print despite the beat — FY26 capex $55.7B (above the ~$50B guide) and margin/funding concerns
earnings.watch: OCI backlog/RPO conversion, the capex trajectory and whether gross margins recover as data centres ramp.

facts.text: Oracle reported Q4 FY26 on 10 Jun 2026: record revenue $19.2B (+21%), total cloud $9.9B (+47%), OCI/IaaS $5.8B (+93%)
Non-GAAP EPS $2.11 beat consensus ~$1.89; FY27 revenue guided ~$90B with Q1 FY27 cloud +57–63% in constant currency
Shares fell ~5–7% after the print on FY26 capex of $55.7B (above the ~$50B guide) and margin concerns

earnings.tradeStatement: Don't chase Adobe (ADBE) on the post-print dip — Q2 beat with AI-first ARR tripling past $500M, but the stock fell ~5.5% on the CFO's departure layered onto the open CEO search; play the reaction cautiously with a defined-risk re-entry (buffered note, or cash-secured puts into a lower strike), not a naked add, until leadership clarity.
earnings.name: Adobe
earnings.headline: Adobe beat and AI ARR tripled — but a CFO exit sent it down ~5.5%. Don't chase the dip.
earnings.thesis: Adobe reported Q2 FY26 on 11 June: record revenue $6.62B (beating ~$6.46–6.58B), non-GAAP EPS $5.96 (beating ~$5.82–5.94), AI-first ARR tripled YoY past $500M, and full-year targets were raised. Yet the stock fell ~5.5% — CFO Dan Durn's departure (effective 15 June) opened a second top-seat vacancy alongside the ongoing CEO search, and the beats were modest against an elevated bar. Unlike a clean capex-overshoot dip, this selloff carries an unresolved governance overhang. Play the reaction with discipline: don't chase the relief bounce — express a defined-risk re-entry (a buffered note, or cash-secured puts struck lower to get paid to wait), and size up only once leadership clarity lands.

variant.street: Bulls point to tripling AI ARR and raised guidance as proof the AI monetisation is working
variant.us: The operating story is fine, but a CFO+CEO vacuum is a real de-rating risk — wait, don't chase
variant.gap: Market may under-price how long a dual leadership vacancy weighs on the multiple

earnings.changeMyMind: A credible permanent CEO/CFO appointment (removing the overhang), or AI-first ARR growth clearly re-accelerating, would turn this from wait-and-see into an add.

pillars.note: Beat but sold off — the dip is tempting, yet the leadership overhang caps the clean-misprice case.
Sell-side constructive on AI ARR, but the beat was modest vs a high bar and the management vacuum splits views.
Dated, just-landed reaction (11 Jun) — a beat overshadowed by the CFO exit; a clear post-print setup.
The leadership vacuum is unresolved — not a washed-out, contrarian-buy setup.

earnings.result: Beat — record revenue $6.62B (vs ~$6.46–6.58B), non-GAAP EPS $5.96 (vs ~$5.82–5.94); AI-first ARR tripled YoY past $500M; FY targets raised
earnings.reaction: Shares fell ~5.5% after-hours — CFO Dan Durn's exit (effective 15 Jun) atop the open CEO search; beats modest vs a high bar
earnings.watch: Leadership clarity (permanent CFO/CEO), AI-first ARR durability, and whether the de-rating finds support.

facts.text: Adobe reported Q2 FY26 on 11 Jun 2026: record revenue $6.62B (beat ~$6.46–6.58B), non-GAAP EPS $5.96 (beat ~$5.82–5.94)
AI-first ARR tripled YoY to over $500M; full-year revenue and EPS targets were raised
Shares fell ~5.5% after-hours on CFO Dan Durn's departure (effective 15 Jun) atop the CEO search, with beats modest vs elevated expectations

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Long gold (XAU) — accumulate the pullback as strategic ballast; a bet on continued central-bank buying and currency debasement, sized to the protection gap, not a tactical momentum trade.
exEarnings.name: Gold — accumulate the pullback
exEarnings.headline: Gold ~$4,180 on a strong-dollar pullback while central banks keep buying
exEarnings.thesis: Gold sits around $4,180 — roughly 25% below its January record — after a third straight weekly decline as the hawkish Fed and a DXY above 100 weigh on it near-term (it printed a $4,150 low on 19 Jun). The strategic case is unchanged: central banks stay net buyers and every major bank's year-end target sits well above spot. For under-protected books the dollar-driven dip is a better accumulation window in the cleanest debasement hedge — sized to the protection gap, not chased.

variant.street: Every major bank bullish — year-end targets 25-44% above spot; central banks net buyers
variant.us: Same direction — accumulate the pullback into under-protected books

exEarnings.changeMyMind: Central banks turning net sellers for two consecutive quarters, or real yields breaking decisively higher.

pillars.note: Bank targets 25-44% above spot versus gold's normal trailing move — roughly a 1.5-2x asymmetry to the upside.
No single dated catalyst — a structural accumulation on a price pullback.
Every major bank's year-end target sits above spot and central banks are net buyers — sell-side and flow both confirm.

Driver specificity yes (sourced central-bank tonnage and named bank targets), but no variant edge — this is the consensus call, not a differentiated one. A well-supported follow, not a thesis with an edge.
Core to the Gold & Currency house view as strategic ballast.
~25% off the highs with positioning reset — neither washed-out nor crowded; mild fuel.
Below the January high near-term, but the long-term uptrend (above the rising 200-day) is intact — confirms on balance.

macro.detail: ~25% below the 28-Jan record of $5,589; a $4,150 low on 19 Jun on a third straight weekly decline as DXY broke above 100 and the Fed turned hawkish; central banks net +244t in Q1 and +17t in April; China adding for 18 straight months.
macro.watch: The strong dollar / hawkish Fed path as the near-term headwind; central-bank demand as the marginal buyer underpinning the dip.

facts.text: Gold ~$4,180 on 22 Jun (a $4,150 low on 19 Jun), about 25% below the 28-Jan all-time high of $5,589; third consecutive weekly decline on the strong dollar
Central banks net buyers: +244t in Q1 2026, +17t in April; China 18 consecutive months

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Long the power & grid build-out (utilities, gas, nuclear) — a lower-beta way to be long the AI theme that pays contracted income; a bet on structural datacenter electricity demand, not on interest rates.
exEarnings.name: AI power & the grid
exEarnings.headline: Electricity is the binding constraint on AI
exEarnings.thesis: Datacenter electricity demand is on track for roughly 1,050 TWh in 2026 — doubling in a few years — with gas and nuclear the dispatchable supply. Owning the power and grid build-out is the lower-beta way to stay long the AI theme, and it pays contracted income while it compounds.

variant.us: Own the power and grid bottleneck instead — lower-beta, contracted income, still under-owned
variant.gap: The market pays up for compute and underprices the electricity constraint behind it

A structural demand story rather than a single dated event.
IEA data and some sell-side support, but no independent breakout / flow confirmation yet.

Core Power & Infrastructure house view.
Still under-owned versus the chip names — the crowd is clearly not yet with us, which is fuel.
Multi-year rate-base and load growth; the trend is constructive but there's no clean breakout off the moving averages yet — mixed.

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Long high-quality duration — extend from cash / bills into 5–10y govt / IG to lock ~4.4% yields; a bet that carry plus eventual cuts beats cash, accepting mark-to-market risk while the Fed stays hawkish.
exEarnings.name: Quality carry — get paid to wait
exEarnings.headline: The Fed held but turned hawkish — own the carry, not the convexity
exEarnings.thesis: The FOMC held at 3.50-3.75% on 17 June, but the dots turned hawkish in Warsh's first meeting — the median now points to a possible hike and no cuts in 2026, with the easing cycle pushed into 2027 on a 4.2% energy-driven CPI. The 'extend before the cuts' convexity trade is gone for now; the case left is simpler and still good — you are paid ~4.5% in high-quality intermediate bonds to wait, ballast that cash can't give a book with equities at all-time highs. Add duration for income and diversification, not for a near-term rally.

variant.street: The hawkish hold backs up yields; the Street is split between higher-for-longer and a 2027 pivot
variant.us: Own quality carry now — 4.5% is paid ballast; don't bank on near-term cuts
variant.gap: The market may still be too optimistic on the timing of the first cut

exEarnings.changeMyMind: Inflation rolling back toward target (pulling the first cut forward), or the Fed actually hiking — either end of the barbell breaks the carry case.

pillars.note: Earning ~4.5% with cuts now a 2027 story — a carry trade with limited near-term convexity, a modest expected move versus normal bond ranges.
The 17 Jun FOMC has passed; the next dated catalyst (29-30 Jul FOMC, 14 Jul CPI) is weeks out — structural, not imminent.
The hold is sourced fact, but the Street is now split on the path — alignment is weaker than before the meeting.
Quantified, sourced drivers (held 3.50-3.75%, hawkish dots, 4.2% CPI), but mostly the consensus read — a modest variant on cut timing.
Core Fixed Income house view — quality income and ballast, even with the cut delayed.
The crowd is still parked in cash and bills; extending is the contrarian-leaning move.
10Y backed up to ~4.5% on the hawkish hold; the price trend is mixed — neutral-to-confirming for a carry buyer.

macro.metric: FOMC held 3.50-3.75%; hawkish dots, no 2026 cuts
macro.detail: The 16-17 June meeting (Warsh's first as Chair) held the target range, but the dot plot shifted up: the median now implies a possible hike and zero cuts this year, pushing easing into 2027. 10Y backed up to ~4.5%.
macro.watch: The 14 Jul CPI and the 29-30 Jul FOMC — whether the energy-led inflation spike fades or hardens the hawkish path.

facts.text: FOMC held at 3.50-3.75% on 17 June (Warsh's first meeting as Chair) — fourth consecutive hold
The June dot plot turned hawkish: median end-2026 ~3.8%, implying a possible hike and no cuts in 2026; easing pushed to 2027
May CPI re-accelerated to 4.2% y/y on an energy shock (core 2.9%); 10Y near 4.5%

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Buy a ~12-month 10-year Treasury range accrual note — earn an enhanced ~7.5% p.a. coupon (vs ~4.0% on cash) for each day the 10Y yield fixes inside a 4.00–4.90% band; with the 10Y at ~4.5% you are paid to hold the view that a hawkish-hold Fed keeps the long end boxed below the proven 5% ceiling, forgoing the coupon only on a decisive break above 4.90% or below 4.00%.
exEarnings.name: 10Y range accrual
exEarnings.trigger: This week's hawkish FOMC hold (17-Jun, Warsh's first meeting, dot-plot up, cuts pushed toward 2027) caps the 10Y from both sides — no cuts to rally it under 4%, a policy rate anchored on hold to stop it running through 5% — exactly the boxed-in regime a range accrual is paid to harvest, with the 10Y holding ~4.5% mid-range as DXY broke above 100.
exEarnings.headline: The hawkish hold boxes the 10Y below 5% — get paid an enhanced coupon to hold the range
exEarnings.thesis: The FOMC held at 3.50-3.75% on 17 June with hawkish dots (a possible hike, no 2026 cuts, easing pushed to 2027) — a stance that boxes in the 10Y from both sides, with the 10Y at ~4.5% sitting mid-range. The TOP of the band is the harder call and the case is specific: 5% has been a proven ceiling — the 10Y peaked near 5.0% in October 2023 and has failed there on every test since, because at ~5% nominal (~2%+ real) duration is the cheapest in two decades and each approach draws heavy real-money buying — pensions and insurers matching liabilities, foreign reserve managers, and asset allocators rotating out of richly-valued equities. A Fed on hold (its next move a cut, not a hike) means the long end has no hiking cycle to un-anchor it, so it cannot sustainably run through 5% without the front end leading. The BOTTOM (~4.00%) is held by sticky inflation — May CPI re-accelerated to 4.2% (core 2.9%) on the energy shock — plus no cuts until 2027 to fuel a rally and an elevated term premium on heavy Treasury supply, all of which keep a floor under long yields. So a 12-month 10Y range accrual pays an enhanced ~7.5% p.a. coupon (vs ~4.0% on cash) for each day the yield fixes inside 4.00–4.90%: you are paid to hold the boxed-in view, with the coupon forgone only on the days it breaks out. Capital is returned at par at maturity (issuer credit aside) — more than cash without an outright duration bet.

variant.street: Bears see fiscal/supply pushing the 10Y toward 5%; bulls wait for cuts to pull it under 4% — both expect a break
variant.us: Neither break holds: 5% is a proven buyer's ceiling and ~4% a sticky-inflation floor — monetise the range rather than bet on the break
variant.gap: The market pays up for the tail break in either direction and under-prices the 10Y simply staying boxed around 4.5%

exEarnings.changeMyMind: A decisive break of the box — a growth scare or sharp disinflation that pulls the 10Y below 4.00% (cuts brought forward), or a fiscal/supply/inflation shock that drives it through 5.00% — either zeros the accrual.

pillars.note: An enhanced coupon (~7.5% vs ~4% cash) for the 10Y simply staying boxed in — a steady carry pickup with capped, income-style upside rather than an outright duration bet.
Dated, landed catalyst — the 17 Jun FOMC hawkish hold (dots up, cuts to 2027) that caps the 10Y from both ends.
The higher-for-longer, range-bound-10Y read is broadly shared, but the precise 4.00–4.90% band and the coupon edge are an estimated structuring call.

Sourced drivers (held 3.50-3.75%, hawkish dots, cuts to 2027, 4.2% CPI, 10Y ~4.5%) plus a specific two-sided case: a proven 5% buyer's ceiling above and a sticky-CPI / supply term-premium floor below.
Core Fixed Income / income house view — paid carry from a boxed-in long end, no outright duration call.
The crowd is parked in cash and bills or positioned for a 10Y break either way; selling 10Y rate-range optionality for an enhanced coupon is the contrarian income move.
The 10Y at ~4.5% mid-range, repeatedly rejected at 5% and bid toward 4% — a well-defined range, the accrual's friend.

macro.metric: FOMC held 3.50-3.75%; hawkish dots; 10Y ~4.5%, capped at 5%
macro.detail: 12-month 10Y Treasury range accrual: ~4.00–4.90% band, ~7.5% p.a. enhanced coupon vs ~4.0% on cash, A-rated issuer, capital at par. Coupon accrues daily for each in-range fixing; forgone on a break above 4.90% or below 4.00%.
macro.watch: The 14 Jul CPI, the 29-30 Jul FOMC and Treasury supply/auctions — a shock that drives the 10Y through 5% or under 4% and zeros the accrual.

facts.text: FOMC held at 3.50-3.75% on 17 June (Warsh's first meeting as Chair); the June dots turned hawkish — a possible hike, no 2026 cuts, easing pushed to 2027
The 10Y Treasury yield backed up to ~4.5% on the hawkish hold; it last peaked near 5.0% in Oct 2023 and has failed at that level on every test since
May CPI re-accelerated to 4.2% y/y (core 2.9%) on an energy shock, keeping an inflation/term premium in long yields

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Long energy majors (XOM / SHEL) — a tactical hedge that the Hormuz risk premium is underpriced after Brent fell to ~$79; a bet on a re-escalation tail, held in income-paying majors, not a structural oil-bull call.
exEarnings.name: Energy — tactical on Hormuz risk
exEarnings.headline: Brent ~$79 as the Iran framework reopens Hormuz — but Geneva talks just stalled
exEarnings.thesis: Brent has steadied around $79 (down ~8% on the week, ~37% off the ~$126 April war-peak) after an interim US-Iran peace framework signed 18 Jun began reopening the Strait of Hormuz and restoring Iranian exports. The geopolitical premium has largely deflated — but Geneva talks to harden the framework were abruptly postponed on 20 Jun, so the re-escalation tail is still live. Energy majors offer a tactical, income-paying hedge against the deal breaking down — explicitly off the standing house view, a trade rather than a strategic tilt.
exEarnings.offThemeWhy: There is no standing desk theme on oil — this is a tactical, event-driven idea off Middle-East supply risk, sized as a hedge, not a strategic house view.

variant.street: Prices the interim US-Iran deal as durable; the geopolitical premium has largely deflated
variant.us: Hold a cheap re-escalation hedge — the market may be too relaxed on the Hormuz tail with the deal only interim
variant.gap: Geopolitical tail risk underpriced now that the premium has bled out

exEarnings.changeMyMind: The interim US-Iran deal hardening into a durable settlement that keeps the Strait of Hormuz open, removing the re-escalation tail.

pillars.note: A ceasefire breakdown could retrace much of the move from ~$126 — a large potential move versus oil's normal range.
Headline-driven — hinges on the Hormuz / Iran path rather than a dated event.
Off-theme tactical; no standing sell-side alignment behind it.

Sourced price drivers (Brent ~$80 off the ~$126 April peak) and a genuine variant — bet the market has over-priced the interim framework as durable, especially with Geneva talks stalled. Driver and variant yes.
Off-theme — explicitly a tactical hedge, not a strategic house view.
The geopolitical premium has largely deflated — positioning two-sided, no clear edge.
Brent has rolled hard off the April spike toward three-month lows — chart bearish near-term, which is the point of a contrarian hedge.

macro.detail: Down ~8% on the week and ~37% off the ~$126 April war-peak (a 4-year high) as an interim US-Iran framework reopened the Strait of Hormuz and restored Iranian exports; near-term range ~$75-82, still headline-sensitive after Geneva talks were postponed on 20 Jun.
macro.watch: Whether the interim US-Iran framework hardens (Geneva talks resuming), OPEC+ supply signals, and any Hormuz flare-up.

facts.text: Brent ~$79 on 22 Jun, down ~8% on the week and ~37% off the ~$126 peak on 30 April (a 4-year high during the Iran war)
An interim US-Iran peace framework (18 Jun) reopened the Strait of Hormuz; Geneva talks to harden it were abruptly postponed on 20 Jun, keeping a re-escalation tail

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Reduce / hedge USD exposure in EUR- and GBP-base books — effectively short the dollar against the home currency — a bet that the post-peak USD weakens into 2026 as the Fed-ECB spread narrows; this is NOT a call on any single pair's spot level, nor specifically a yen- or euro-strength view.
exEarnings.name: Hedge the USD concentration
exEarnings.headline: Dollar broke DXY 100 on the hawkish hold — a prime window to hedge USD-heavy non-dollar books
exEarnings.thesis: The hawkish 17-Jun FOMC hold and the 4.2% May CPI have driven the dollar higher, with DXY breaking above 100 and EUR/USD down to ~1.143 — even as every 2026 bank outlook still calls a post-peak dollar and a narrowing Fed-ECB spread that should weaken it into next year. For EUR- and GBP-base books carrying outsized USD sleeves, this stronger level is the window to hedge the currency mismatch — currency-hedged share classes for Retail, FX forwards or zero-cost collars for Professionals — sized to the mismatch, not run as a directional bet.

variant.street: Major-bank consensus is a post-peak dollar — more USD weakness into 2026 as the Fed-ECB rate spread narrows
variant.us: Same direction; use the hawkish-hold firmness to put hedges on USD-heavy non-dollar books at a better level
variant.gap: The near-term firm dollar is a hedging gift, not a reason to stay unhedged

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Earn the carry on the wide US rate differential — effectively long the high-yielding USD versus a low-yielder (JPY / EUR) via dual-currency deposits — a bet that the differential persists and the pair stays range-bound so you collect the coupon; it is NOT a directional spot view, and the worst case is converting into the currency you'd have bought anyway.
exEarnings.name: FX carry & dual-currency income
exEarnings.headline: Monetise the wide rate differential — FX carry and dual-currency income
exEarnings.thesis: With the front end held high by the hawkish Fed and rate differentials wide, income books can be paid to express a range view on pairs they are happy to hold either side of. Dual-currency deposits and currency-hedged carry turn the differential into an enhanced coupon — you accept conversion at a set rate in return for yield. A Professional, range-bound income tool, not a directional dollar bet.

variant.street: Differentials favour the dollar now; a yen and euro recovery is a 2026 story as the gap narrows
variant.us: Earn the carry while the differential is wide, on pairs you would hold either side of
variant.gap: Get paid to wait for the FX moves the house expects later

exEarnings.changeMyMind: A fast, disorderly dollar reversal — e.g. an abrupt dovish Fed pivot — that compresses the differential and collapses the carry.

pillars.note: Carry is steady coupon income with a defined conversion risk — low, bounded asymmetry by design.
Soft and ongoing: the hawkish hold keeps the front end and the differential wide, but there is no single dated trigger.
The wide US rate differential is a hard, confirmable macro fact and the income angle sits squarely in the house view.

On the Gold & Currency house view — currency diversification and income from the rate differential.

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Long short-dated USD/JPY puts — buy ~156-strike 4–6wk puts (~1% premium) to play a yen-strength snapback: 158 is in MoF-intervention territory and the BoJ is set to hike late June. Entry ~158, take profit / unwind into 153–154, cut if spot breaks back above ~161.
exEarnings.name: USD/JPY downside
exEarnings.trigger: Still invalid on the 22-Jun reopen: the catalyst this trade was built around has passed and resolved AGAINST it — the BoJ hiked 25bp to 1% on 17 Jun, yet USD/JPY ran UP to a 160.89 high on 19 Jun (weakest yen since Jul-2024) despite ~$70bn+ of MoF intervention, and spot is still pinned ~160.8. The wide differential and carry trade are dominant; there is no live justification for owning one-way yen-strength downside here — the two-sided strangle is the right way to own intervention risk. Held back.

exEarnings.headline: USD/JPY ~158 into a late-June BoJ hike — own short-dated puts for the yen snapback
exEarnings.thesis: USD/JPY sits around 158 — historically MoF/BoJ intervention territory — held up by a wide US-Japan differential after the hawkish 17-Jun Fed hold. But the BoJ is expected to raise rates later in June, and intervention risk rises the higher spot grinds. This is a classic tactical short-dated downside trade: buy ~156-strike puts for a few weeks, defined premium at risk, geared to a fast yen-strength snapback. Take profit into 153–154; cut if the differential wins and spot breaks back above ~161. Invalidated by a dovish BoJ hold plus a fresh hawkish-Fed leg that re-widens the gap.
exEarnings.offThemeWhy: Tactical, event-driven FX trade around the late-June BoJ — a short-dated directional position, not the desk's strategic currency-diversification house view.

exEarnings.headline: ECB just hiked to 2.25% — a defined-risk EUR/USD call spread for euro catch-up
exEarnings.thesis: The ECB raised its policy rate 25bp to 2.25% on 11 June as euro-area inflation ran near 3.0%, while the Fed's hawkish hold keeps it on pause — the Fed-ECB spread is set to narrow, the core of the post-peak-dollar / euro-recovery view. With EUR/USD around 1.17 and 2026 outlooks clustering at 1.15–1.19 (and seasonality eyeing 1.18+), express the upside cheaply and with capped risk: a 1.18/1.21 call spread for a few weeks. Max loss is the ~0.7% net premium; target 1.20–1.21. Invalidated by a hawkish-Fed-driven risk-off that re-bids the dollar and pulls EUR/USD under ~1.155.
exEarnings.offThemeWhy: Tactical post-ECB directional trade — a short-dated, defined-risk expression of the euro-recovery lean, not the standing house view.

Entry ~1.34, target 1.37–1.38, unwind/cut if spot breaks 1.315 (you are put the pair at the short strike).
exEarnings.name: GBP/USD upside
exEarnings.trigger: Still not met on the 22-Jun reopen: this bullish risk reversal needs cable to break above ~1.345 on a soft-dollar leg, but GBP/USD is ~1.32 with DXY above 100 on the hawkish Fed. It needs a soft-dollar turn — the opposite of today's dollar-melt-up tape — so there is no live justification to put on geared cable upside now.
exEarnings.headline: Cable ~1.34 with the BoE on hold at 3.75% — a near-zero-cost bullish risk reversal
exEarnings.thesis: GBP/USD trades around 1.34 with the Bank of England holding Bank Rate at 3.75% — a relatively high carry against a Fed that is on pause and a post-peak, range-bound dollar. With forecasts spanning ~1.33–1.40, express the bullish lean efficiently: a near-zero-cost risk reversal — sell the 1.31 put to fund the 1.37 call. You get geared upside above 1.37; the risk is real — you are long the pair at 1.31 if cable sells off, so this is for books happy to own GBP lower. Target 1.37–1.38; unwind if spot breaks 1.315. Invalidated by a hawkish-Fed dollar surge or a dovish BoE turn.
exEarnings.offThemeWhy: Tactical, carry-supported cable trade — a short-dated geared expression, not the strategic currency house view.

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Long USD/JPY volatility at the intervention line — buy a 2–4wk strangle (~157 put / ~164 call, ~1.3% total premium) to own the next move either way: fresh MoF intervention or a policy/jawbone shock could snap it sharply lower, while a dominant carry trade could squeeze it higher through 162. Entry ~160.8, take profit on a break beyond 157 or 164, let it decay / cut if pinned 159–162 (theta).
exEarnings.name: USD/JPY event volatility
exEarnings.trigger: Still live on the 22-Jun reopen: USD/JPY is holding ~160.8 after printing a 160.89 high on 19 Jun — the weakest yen since 11-Jul-2024 and deep in MoF-intervention territory — even after the BoJ's 17-Jun hike to 1% and ~$70bn+ of intervention failed to arrest it; with Tokyo 'ready to act at any time', the next move is a genuinely two-sided binary (a further carry-driven melt-up vs a sharp intervention/jawbone snapback), so owning the move either way still has a live justification today.
exEarnings.headline: USD/JPY ~160.8 in the intervention zone — own the two-sided move with a short-dated strangle
exEarnings.thesis: USD/JPY has broken to ~160.80 — the weakest yen since Jul-2024 and squarely in MoF-intervention territory — after the BoJ's 17-Jun hike to 1% and ~$70bn+ of intervention failed to arrest the slide and the wide differential keeps the carry trade dominant. The next move is genuinely two-sided: with Japan 'ready to act at any time', fresh intervention or a jawbone/policy shock could snap it sharply lower, while if the carry keeps winning it squeezes higher through 162. When you are confident in a big move but not its sign, own the volatility — a 2–4wk strangle (~157 put / ~164 call) profits on a break beyond either wing, with the two premiums as the defined max loss. Take profit on a break beyond 157 or 164; let it decay/expire if spot stays pinned 159–162.
exEarnings.offThemeWhy: Tactical long-volatility trade around live intervention risk at the highs — non-directional, not the strategic currency house view.

exEarnings.thesis: EUR/USD has broken below 1.15 into the 1.14 handle (~1.143) as the hawkish 17-Jun Fed hold (dots now pencilling a 2026 hike) and a 4.2% May CPI drove DXY above 100 — overwhelming the 11-Jun ECB hike to 2.25%. The strategic house view still calls a post-peak dollar and a euro recovery later in 2026; near-term, though, the melt-up is in control. A short-dated, defined-risk expression of today's tape, sized as a trade, not a tilt.
